segments.title: HMS Tiger as a battlecruiser hunter
segments.summary: The opening argument is that HMS Tiger's unusual six-inch secondary battery made sense for a battlecruiser intended to pursue comparable German ships.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames Tiger as an unusual British battlecruiser: not merely a fast capital ship, but one equipped to hunt enemy battlecruisers. That proposed role explains why her secondary armament matters to more than destroyer defence. The game reconstruction is used to test how that design logic might work in a duel.

evidence.note: Tiger is introduced as a battlecruiser designed to kill battlecruisers.

segments.title: Tiger's mixed-fuel machinery and protection
segments.summary: A reconstruction of Tiger's machinery connects mixed oil-and-coal firing, turbines, bulkheads, steering, and underwater protection to her performance.
segments.body: The video models Tiger as a roughly 28,500-ton ship using water-tube boilers and steam turbines, with both oil and coal in her fuel arrangement. Dr. Clarke also points to substantial bulkheads, a double bottom, steering arrangements, and anti-torpedo protection. These details show why speed and survivability were linked design decisions rather than separate specifications.

evidence.note: The reconstruction discusses displacement, boilers, mixed fuel, turbines, bulkheads, steering, and protection.

segments.title: Main battery, wing turrets, and three funnels
segments.summary: Tiger's 13.5-inch guns, wing-turret arrangement, and three funnels reveal the spatial compromises behind the ship's distinctive silhouette.
segments.body: The game model substitutes twin 14-inch turrets for Tiger's 13.5-inch weapons, but retains the challenge of placing wing turrets and three funnels. Dr. Clarke treats the layout as a useful reminder that a capital ship's fighting power had to coexist with machinery spaces and a workable hull form. The result is recognisably Tiger-like without claiming a perfect simulation.

evidence.note: The video discusses the 13.5-inch battery, wing turrets, and the difficulty of fitting three funnels.

segments.title: Speed, endurance, armour, and the six-inch battery
segments.summary: Conflicting speed figures and Tiger's armour scheme lead into the key feature: twelve single six-inch guns, six on a broadside.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares published figures before favouring a 29-knot maximum speed and gives several endurance figures at different speeds. He then sets Tiger's belt, deck, and conning-tower protection beside the armament discussion. Twelve single six-inch guns gave her six guns on either broadside, a battery central to the ship's intended tactical role.

evidence.note: The transcript covers competing speed sources, range, armour values, and Tiger's twelve six-inch guns.

segments.title: The German battlecruiser Tiger was meant to meet
segments.summary: A German opponent based on Moltke or Seydlitz illustrates the 11-inch-gun battlecruisers Tiger was expected to confront.
segments.body: Tiger is described as an improved Queen Mary whose likely targets included German contemporaries such as Moltke, rather than a generic opponent. The comparison highlights the German preference for 11-inch guns as a weight-efficient compromise and the different turret and funnel arrangement of the German ships. It turns a ship-design exercise into a comparison of national solutions to the same tactical problem.

evidence.note: Tiger is compared with Moltke and a Seydlitz-like German design armed with 11-inch guns.

segments.title: Why Tiger carried six-inch secondaries
segments.summary: Four-inch secondaries suit destroyer defence; Tiger's six-inch guns are presented as weapons for fighting larger opponents at closer ranges.
segments.body: The comparison makes the armament distinction explicit: the German four-inch secondaries are useful against destroyers, while Tiger's six-inch guns offer more against vessels larger than destroyers. Dr. Clarke argues that this was the practical expression of Tiger's battlecruiser-hunting purpose. He also notes that her flagship facilities made her a more natural battlecruiser-force flagship than Repulse.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts four-inch and six-inch secondaries and relates Tiger's fit to her flagship role.

segments.title: A counterfactual Tiger in the Second World War
segments.summary: Tiger's staff spaces and useful armament make her an intriguing hypothetical flagship for hunting raiders in places such as the South Atlantic.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke suggests that, with suitable modifications, Tiger could have been valuable as a Second World War flagship even without the heaviest guns. Her staff accommodation and ability to threaten smaller opponents would have suited independent hunting duties. In the South Atlantic, he argues, that could have released County-class cruisers for the Mediterranean.

evidence.note: A hypothetical Second World War role connects Tiger's flagship facilities to raider hunting and cruiser allocation.

segments.title: Fire control and the 13.5-inch guns
segments.summary: Tiger's two fire directors, pre-Jutland elevation improvement, gun range, and firing rate show how gunnery performance depended on more than calibre.
segments.body: The video describes Tiger's two directors and the possibility of coordinating them for improved control, depending on the command team. Just before Jutland, super-elevating prisms enabled the director-controlled turrets to use the guns' full elevation. The 13.5-inch weapons fired 1,400-pound projectiles to a stated maximum range of about 23,740 yards, normally at roughly two rounds per minute.

evidence.note: The transcript covers directors, super-elevating prisms, projectile weight, range, and rate of fire.

segments.title: Closing to six-inch range means accepting torpedo risk
segments.summary: The six-inch battery could be more reliable than heavy guns at extreme range, but exploiting it required closing into the danger zone for torpedoes.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes theoretical maximum gun range from the much harder task of scoring hits there. At six-inch range, a large battlecruiser with good rangefinding could expect a more useful volume of fire against its target. The trade-off was stark: closing to exploit that battery also brought the ship within torpedo range, as the simulated fight demonstrates.

evidence.note: The transcript links hit probability at range, six-inch fire, closing, and the danger of torpedoes.

segments.title: Tiger's role in a layered battle fleet
segments.summary: A battlecruiser did not need to destroy every opponent alone if it could damage and slow one for the heavier ships following behind.
segments.body: The video argues that Royal Navy battlecruiser thinking assumed depth behind the fast ships. Tiger might damage an opponent, force it to withdraw, or slow it with gunfire or torpedoes before Queen Elizabeth-class battleships arrived to finish the action. This frames battlecruiser value as a fleet-level problem rather than a demand for self-sufficient victory in every duel.

evidence.note: The transcript explains how Tiger could damage an enemy for following cruisers or Queen Elizabeth-class battleships.

segments.title: Evaluate ships as parts of fleets, not isolated objects
segments.summary: The closing lesson is a method for naval history: judge a ship by the fleet capability it supports, not just by its individual specifications.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that a ship should be understood as a component of a fleet rather than as a stand-alone technical object. A navy choosing an aircraft carrier is choosing organic aviation support; the same logic applies to battlecruisers and their place in fleet construction. That perspective turns questions about armour, guns, and speed into questions about the wider force a navy was trying to build.

evidence.note: The closing discussion explains fleet construction and the need to evaluate ships in their wider force context.
